summary: Epic He's Gone → Truckin' jam, soaring Bird Song and tight jamming
blurb: The show delivers a monster He’s Gone → Truckin’ → The Other One sequence plus a soaring Bird Song and stellar China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider transition. Jerry’s guitar shines throughout while Phil lays down a thunderous bass solo in Truckin’. A top‑shelf soundboard capture makes this a must‑listen performance.
review: On June 22, 1973 at Vancouver’s P.N.E. Coliseum the Dead rolled out one of their most adventurous mid‑’70 shows. The first set eases in with classics like Box of Rain and Bird Song before launching into an inspired China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider segue that showcases Bob Weir’s blistering lead solo and Keith Godchaux’s jazzy organ textures.  The second set is where the night truly soars: a monster He’s Gone → Truckin’ → The Other One jam builds into an epic crescendo, highlighted by Phil Lesh’s legendary bass solo in Truckin’.  Bird Song rises to one of its highest peaks of the era, and the closing stretch—Wharf Rat into Sugar Magnolia—maintains the momentum through a seamless flow.  Across all available sources the band is described as “locked‑in,” with Jerry Garcia’s tone both rich and fluid, Phil’s low‑end thunderous, Bob’s rhythm rock solid, and Keith’s piano/organ bright and prominent.  The Miller soundboard capture (gd1973-06-22.sbd.miller.88526.sbeok.flac16) delivers crystal‑clear fidelity, preserving the nuance of each instrument while minimizing the occasional hiccup noted on other versions.  For Deadheads seeking a definitive listening experience this show offers legendary improvisation, tight ensemble playing, and an audio quality that lets every nuance shine.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All nine analyses are overwhelmingly positive, repeatedly calling the performance 'legendary', 'epic' and 'stellar'. Multiple recordings highlight the same standout jams (He’s Gone → Truckin’ → The Other One, Bird Song, China Cat Sunflower) with no consistent criticism of playing quality. The only minor negatives concern isolated songs like Bertha, which does not detract from the overall show, justifying a strong positive sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear soundboard capture with excellent mix, minimal artifacts, and the most complete representation of the epic jams.

key_highlights: Monster He’s Gone → Truckin’ → The Other One jam that defines the second set.
Soaring Bird Song highlighted as one of the best performances of the era.
Legendary China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider transition with Bob’s standout solo.
Marathon Truckin’ → The Other One → Sugar Magnolia sequence delivering nonstop energy.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s tone is rich and fluid, delivering soaring leads on Bird Song, China Cat Sunflower and the massive He’s Gone‑Truckin’‑Other One jam.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh provides a thunderous low end with a legendary bass solo in Truckin’, bomb‑filled fills throughout, and drives the extended improvisations.
band_performance.Bob: Weir offers solid rhythm work and an inspired lead solo on China Cat Sunflower that many reviewers cite as one of his best.
band_performance.Keith: Godchaux’s piano/organ is prominent and jazzy, shining especially on Bird Song, China Cat Sunflower and the transitions between jams.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann’s drumming is tight, jazz‑inflected and propels the long second‑set jams with steady energy.
